title.value: OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning
title.evidence: # OTC: Optimal Tool Calls via Reinforcement Learning

authors.evidence: Hongru Wangα, Cheng $\mathbf { Q i a n } ^ {  eta }$, Wanjun Zhongδ, Xiusi $\mathbf { C h e n } ^ {  eta }$, Jiahao ${  f Q } {  f i } {  f u } ^ { \sigma }$, Shijue Huangµ, Bowen $\mathbf { J i n } ^ {  eta }$, Mengdi Wangσ, Kam-Fai $\mathbf { W o n g } ^ { \alpha }$, Heng $\mathbf { J i } ^ {  eta }$

value: The Chinese University of Hong Kong
Hong Kong University of Science and Technology
affiliations.evidence: αThe Chinese University of Hong Kong, βUniversity of Illinois Urbana-Champaign σPrinceton University, $^ \delta \mathsf { S u n }$ Yat-sen University, $\mu$ Hong Kong University of Science and Technology

current_issues.value: Existing reinforcement learning methods often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage, leading to excessive tool calls or insufficient tool use.
current_issues.evidence: While reinforcement learning (RL) has shown promise in improving TIR by optimizing final answer correctness, existing approaches often overlook the efficiency and cost associated with tool usage.

target.value: Develop a method to optimize tool usage in tool-integrated reasoning, balancing correctness and efficiency.
target.evidence: We aim to develop such a method based on a foundational assumption: for each question and each model, there exists an optimal number of tool calls, defined as the minimal number required for the model to arrive at a correct answer.

framework_innovation.value: The OTC-PO framework introduces a tool-integrated reward that optimizes for both correctness and tool usage efficiency, promoting high tool productivity.
framework_innovation.evidence: Our method introduces a tool-integrated reward that jointly considers correctness and tool efficiency, promoting high tool productivity.
framework_innovation.notes: OTC-PO is the first RL framework to explicitly optimize tool-use efficiency.

metric_innovation.value: Introduced tool productivity (TP) as a metric to measure the effectiveness and efficiency of tool calls during inference.
metric_innovation.evidence: We additionally define a new metric, tool productivity (TP), which measures the effectiveness and efficiency of tool calls during inference.
metric_innovation.notes: TP reflects how efficiently the model converts tool usage into correct answers.

algorithm_innovation.value: OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO algorithms integrate optimal tool call control into existing reinforcement learning frameworks.
algorithm_innovation.evidence: We instantiate this framework within both Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and Group Relative Preference Optimization (GRPO), resulting in OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO.
algorithm_innovation.notes: These algorithms allow for flexible integration into existing RL systems.

performance_improvement.value: OTC-PO reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4% while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.
performance_improvement.evidence: Experiments with Qwen-2.5 and Qwen-Math across multiple QA benchmarks show that our approach reduces tool calls by up to 73.1% and improves tool productivity by up to 229.4%, while maintaining comparable answer accuracy.

tool_call_reduction.value: Significant reduction in tool calls achieved without sacrificing accuracy.
tool_call_reduction.evidence: OTC-PPO achieves significant improvement in terms of TC and TP compared with Search-R1, and OTC-GRPO can further boost the performance due to more accurate approximation.

model_size_effect.value: Larger models tend to over-rely on external tool calls unless explicitly penalized for such behavior.
model_size_effect.evidence: As language models scale up, they tend to over-rely on external tool calls unless explicitly penalized for such behavior.

generalization_performance.value: OTC-PO demonstrates better generalization capabilities across various benchmarks compared to conventional methods.
generalization_performance.evidence: OTC-PPO outperforms the Search-R1-PPO in terms of EM on all benchmarks with Qwen2.5-7B-Base model with much less tool calls.

case_study_insights.value: OTC-PO effectively reduces unnecessary tool calls while maximizing internal reasoning capabilities.
case_study_insights.evidence: Our proposed method not only minimize external tool calls, but also maximize internal reasoning capabilities of LLMs at the same time.

task_definition.value: The goal is to generate the correct answer with the minimal number of tool calls required for the task.
task_definition.evidence: The objective of the task is to generate the correct answer $\hat { a }$ with the minimal cost of tools in the full trajectory.

base_reward.value: Traditional correctness reward based on whether the predicted answer matches the ground truth.
base_reward.evidence: Most prior predominantly focuses on rule-based correctness rewards.

efficiency_reward.value: Introduces a tool-integrated reward that considers both correctness and tool usage efficiency.
efficiency_reward.evidence: We regard the tool-integrated reward $r _ { t o o l }$ as a coefficient of conventional reward function.

final_reward.value: The final reward combines the correctness reward with the tool usage efficiency reward to promote optimal tool calls.
final_reward.evidence: The final tool-integrated reward function is $r _ { \phi } ^ { t o o l } ( q , y ) = \alpha * r _ { t o o l } * r _ { \phi } ( q , y )$.

algorithm_integration.value: OTC-PO can be integrated with existing RL algorithms with minimal changes.
algorithm_integration.evidence: OTC-PO is lightweight and broadly applicable, requiring only minimal changes to standard RL pipelines.

datasets.value: NQ and HotpotQA for search tasks; ToRL dataset for code tasks.
datasets.evidence: We mainly follow the Search-R1 and ToRL setting and use same baselines and datasets for the fair and comprehensive evaluation.

models.evidence: Using multiple LLMs—including Qwen-2.5-3B/7B-Base and Qwen2.5-Math-1.5B/7B-Base.

parameters.value: Maximal tool calls set to 4 for Search-R1 and 3 for ToRL.
parameters.evidence: We set the max turn (maximal number of tool calls) in Search-R1 (ToRL) to 4 (3).

resources.evidence: We conduct our experiments on 8 A100-80G GPU.

search_tool_results.value: OTC-PPO and OTC-GRPO show significant reductions in tool calls and improvements in tool productivity.
search_tool_results.evidence: OTC-PPO achieves significant improvement in terms of TC and TP compared with Search-R1.

code_tool_results.value: Preliminary results indicate similar findings in code tasks as with search tasks.
code_tool_results.evidence: We already observe similar findings as mentioned above.
code_tool_results.notes: More results will be released soon.

behavior_analysis.value: OTC-PO reduces unnecessary tool calls while allowing models to leverage internal reasoning effectively.
behavior_analysis.evidence: Our proposed method not only minimizes external tool calls, but also maximizes internal reasoning capabilities of LLMs.
